_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 7-10 Helmet Removal (Revised 1/27/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan Procedures Protocol Section 7-10: Helmet Removal — MDHHS Approved 1/27/2023
purpose: Govern prehospital helmet removal decisions based on helmet type and presence of concurrent shoulder pads. Maintain constant spinal precautions throughout the process. Airway control always takes priority.

sections.title: High-Impact Helmets (Always Remove)
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 7-10: Helmet Removal — High Impact
points: HIGH-IMPACT helmets (motorcycle, automobile racing, off-road motorsports): ALWAYS REMOVE the helmet
These helmets prevent adequate airway assessment and management if left in place
Two-rescuer technique: one rescuer stabilizes the head/neck while second removes the helmet
Maintain manual inline cervical spinal stabilization throughout removal
Apply cervical collar and continue spinal precautions per Spinal Precautions-Procedure Protocol after removal

sections.title: Low-Impact Helmets WITH Concurrent Shoulder Pads
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 7-10: Helmet Removal — Low Impact With Shoulder Pads
points: Examples: football, ice hockey, lacrosse (helmets worn WITH shoulder pads)
Prearrangement option — if the patient is AWAKE and protecting their own airway: leave helmet in place AND remove the face shield/faceguard to access the airway
If patient is unconscious or unable to protect airway: leave helmet in place AND remove the face shield/faceguard IMMEDIATELY
If the face shield/faceguard CANNOT be removed: remove both the helmet AND shoulder pads simultaneously
If the airway cannot be controlled with the helmet in place: remove the helmet AND shoulder pads simultaneously
When removing both simultaneously: one rescuer stabilizes the head, second rescuer removes shoulder pads, third removes helmet — coordinated action to maintain neutral spinal alignment

sections.title: Low-Impact Helmets WITHOUT Shoulder Pads
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 7-10: Helmet Removal — Low Impact Without Shoulder Pads
points: Examples: bicycle helmets, baseball/softball batting helmets, skateboard helmets
ALWAYS REMOVE the helmet
Standard two-rescuer helmet removal technique with manual inline spinal stabilization
Apply cervical collar after removal and continue spinal precautions

sections.title: General Principles
points: Maintain constant spinal precautions throughout the entire process
Airway management takes priority over helmet removal decision-making
Never remove a helmet without maintaining neutral spinal alignment
After helmet removal: apply cervical collar; continue spinal precautions per Spinal Precautions-Procedure Protocol
Document reason for helmet removal or retention in PCR

deterioration_flags: Airway compromise with helmet in place — remove helmet regardless of type; airway takes priority
Face shield cannot be removed (low-impact with shoulder pads) — remove helmet and shoulder pads simultaneously
Patient unconscious with low-impact helmet and shoulder pads — remove face shield immediately; leave helmet unless airway cannot be controlled
scope_notes: Low-impact WITH shoulder pads: leave helmet + remove face shield if airway can be controlled (prearrangement); remove both if face shield cannot be removed or airway cannot be controlled
Low-impact WITHOUT shoulder pads (bicycle, baseball): ALWAYS remove
Airway control always supersedes helmet decision — if airway cannot be managed with helmet in place, remove it
